title: CSS Foundations: Zero to Mastery
content:
# 🎨 CSS Foundations: Zero to Mastery If HTML is the "Skeleton" of your website, **CSS (Cascading Style Sheets)** is the skin, hair, and clothing. Without CSS, every website on the internet would look like a plain black-and-white word document. In this lesson, we break down exactly how to paint your website from the ground up. --- ## 🎯 Learning Objectives - Understand the three ways to link CSS to your HTML. - Master the logic of **Tags, Classes, and IDs**. - Learn how to control the "Box" around every element. - Use **Relative Units** for designs that don't break on small screens. - Calculate **Specificity** to solve style conflicts. --- ## 1️⃣ How to Connect CSS to HTML There are three ways to tell a browser how to style your page. We move from the simplest to the most professional method. ### Method A: Inline CSS (The Quick Fix) You write the CSS directly inside an HTML tag using the `style` attribute. ```html

Hello World

``` *Cons: If you have 100 pages, you have to change every single tag manually. Professionals almost never use this.* ### Method B: Internal CSS (The Style Tag) You write your CSS inside a ` ``` *Cons: It only works for that one specific page. Still difficult to maintain across a large website.* ### Method C: External CSS (The Professional Choice) You create a completely separate file called `styles.css` and link it to your HTML. ```html ``` **Why?** You can style 1,000 pages with a single file. It makes your website load faster and keeps your code organized. --- ## 2️⃣ The Anatomy of a CSS Rule A CSS rule is made of three parts: 1. **Selector**: Which element am I targeting? 2. **Property**: What do I want to change (color, font, size)? 3. **Value**: What is the new setting? ```css /* Selector Property Value */ h1 { color: blue; } ``` *Note: Always use braces `{}` to wrap your styles and a semicolon `;` at the end of every value.* --- ## 3️⃣ The Big Three Selectors To style something, you must "select" it. There are three primary ways to do this: ### 1. The Tag Selector Targets every element of a certain type (e.g., all `

` tags). ```css p { font-size: 16px; } ``` ### 2. The Class Selector (The Dot `.`) This is the most common way to style. You give an HTML element a `class` name, and call it in CSS with a dot. **HTML:** `

` **CSS:** `.card { background: white; }` ### 3. The ID Selector (The Hash `#`) IDs are unique. You should only use an ID once per page. Call it in CSS with a hash. **HTML:** `` **CSS:** `#submit-btn { border: none; }` --- ## 4️⃣ Relationships: Combinators & State ### Descendants (Parent & Child) You can target elements inside other elements by using a space. ```css .card h1 { color: black; } /* Targets only h1 tags INSIDE a element with class .card */ ``` ### Pseudo-Classes (The State) What if you want to change a button's color ONLY when the mouse is over it? We use **`:hover`**. ```css button:hover { background: gold; } ``` --- ## 5️⃣ The Holy Grail: The CSS Box Model Every single thing you see on a webpage is a box. The browser calculates the size of that box using four layers, starting from the inside out: 1. **Content**: Your text or images. 2. **Padding**: Transparent space **inside** the box (pushes content in). 3. **Border**: The wall around the padding. 4. **Margin**: Transparent space **outside** the box (pushes other elements away). ### The "Border-Box" Reset By default, padding and borders make your boxes larger than you intended. Add this to the top of every CSS file to fix it: ```css * { box-sizing: border-box; margin: 0; padding: 0; } ``` --- ## 6️⃣ Units: Absolute vs Relative - **Absolute (`px`)**: Pixels are fixed units. They do not change size on different screens. - **Relative (`rem`, `%`, `vh`)**: These units scale! - **`rem`**: Based on the user's browser font size (Great for accessibility). - **`%`**: Percentage of the parent container. - **`vh` / `vw`**: Percentage of the screen height or width. --- ## 🛠️ Mastery Challenge: The Professional Profile Card **Goal:** Build a styled card from scratch. 1. Link an **External CSS** file to your HTML. 2. Create a `div` with a `class="profile-card"`. 3. Use the **Box Model**: Give it `padding: 2rem`, a `border`, and `margin: 20px auto` to center it. 4. **Typography**: Use `rem` units for your text size. 5. **Interaction**: Use a **`:hover`** state to change the card's background color when the mouse passes over it. 6. **Images**: Use `max-width: 100%` on any image inside the card to ensure it doesn't break the layout. --- ## 🎓 Summary CSS Foundations is about mastering the "Rules of the Box." By understanding how to link your files, selector nomenclature, and the Box Model, you have the power to turn a simple white page into a masterpiece.

questions.question_text: Which method of connecting CSS is considered the 'Professional Standard' for large websites?
questions.explanation: External CSS allows you to maintain multiple pages with a single file and enables browser caching.

questions.question_text: What character is used to select a 'Class' in CSS?
questions.explanation: Example: .card targets an element with class='card'.

questions.question_text: What character is used to select an 'ID' in CSS?
questions.explanation: Example: #header targets an element with id='header'.

questions.question_text: In the CSS Box Model, which layer adds space INSIDE the boundary wall (between content and border)?
questions.explanation: Padding is the 'inner cushion' for your content.

questions.question_text: In the CSS Box Model, which layer adds space OUTSIDE the boundary wall to push other boxes away?
questions.explanation: Margin creates breathing room between different elements.

questions.question_text: How do you apply a style that only triggers when a user moves their mouse over an element?
questions.explanation: The :hover pseudo-class is the standard for mouse-over interactions.

questions.question_text: What is the anatomy of a CSS rule?
questions.explanation: Selector grabs the element, Property is what you change, and Value is the setting.

questions.question_text: Why should you use 'rem' instead of 'px' for font-sizes?
options: Because px is being deleted from CSS
Because rem is relative to user browser settings, making your site more accessible
Because rem is faster to type
questions.explanation: Accessibility is a core responsibility; relative units respect user preferences.

questions.question_text: What does the 'box-sizing: border-box' property do?
options: It adds a red border to every box
It forces padding and borders to be calculated INSIDE the width, making layout math predictable
It removes all images from the page
questions.explanation: This prevents 'box bursting' when you add padding or borders.

questions.question_text: Which tag is used to link an External CSS file inside the <head> of an HTML document?
questions.explanation: <link rel='stylesheet' href='...'> matches the two files together.

questions.question_text: What is the default value of the 'display' property for a <div> element?
questions.explanation: Block elements start on a new line and take 100% of the width.

questions.question_text: What happens if you use 'display: none' on an element?
options: It becomes transparent but keeps its space on the page
It disappears completely and the surrounding layout collapses into its space
It moves to the top of the page
questions.explanation: Display: none removes the element from the layout entirely.

questions.question_text: How do you select only the <h1> tags that are inside a <div> with class '.card'?
questions.explanation: The space (descendant selector) targets items inside other items.

questions.question_text: Which unit represents the percentage of the parent container's width?
questions.explanation: Percentage is relative to the parent box's size.

questions.question_text: What is 'Specificity' in CSS?
options: The mathematical score that decides which style wins when two rules target the same element
The length of your CSS file
questions.explanation: Conflict resolution is handled via specificity scores (IDs > Classes > Tags).

questions.question_text: Which property is used to change the background color of an element?
questions.explanation: color changes text; background-color changes the box fill.

questions.question_text: What is a 'CSS Reset'?
options: A set of rules used to remove default browser styling for consistency across all browsers
questions.explanation: Resets ensure a clean slate so your design looks the same in Chrome, Safari, and Firefox.

questions.question_text: How do you apply a border to an element?
questions.explanation: The border shorthand includes size, style, and color.

questions.question_text: Which relative unit is based specifically on the user's browser default font size?
questions.explanation: REM stands for Root EM (the root being the <html> element).

questions.question_text: True or False: Every HTML element is effectively a rectangle (a Box).
questions.explanation: Everything is calculated as a rectangle, even if border-radius makes it look like a circle.
